findings.title: Client-supplied priceId and URLs passed to Stripe without allowlist validation
findings.description: The `priceId`, `successUrl`, and `cancelUrl` args come from the client and are forwarded directly to the Stripe Checkout API without any server-side validation. A malicious caller could supply an arbitrary Stripe price ID (enabling them to check out at an unintended price point) or craft redirect URLs pointing to phishing pages. Even though the Stripe dashboard can restrict price IDs, the server should enforce an allowlist of known price IDs. The success/cancel URLs should be validated to be absolute HTTPS URLs on the application's own domain.

findings.title: Stripe API error response body leaked to caller
findings.description: When the Stripe API returns a non-OK response, the full `response.text()` body is included verbatim in the thrown error message: `throw new Error('Stripe API error: ' + error)`. Stripe error bodies can include diagnostic information about your account configuration (e.g., key type, feature flags). This text will propagate to the client in the Convex error response.

findings.title: stripe.ts uses raw ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() instead of requireAuth(ctx)
findings.description: CLAUDE.md and the project pattern require all auth checks to use `requireAuth(ctx)` from `convex/lib/auth.ts`. `stripe.ts` uses the legacy `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()` pattern directly. While functionally equivalent today, it diverges from the canonical pattern and will be overlooked during any future migration of `requireAuth`.

findings.title: aggregateQueries.ts uses raw ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() throughout ‚Äî legacy auth pattern
findings.description: Every query handler in `aggregateQueries.ts` (`allFoodTrials`, `foodTrialsByStatus`, `foodTrialByName`, `allWeeklyDigests`, `weeklyDigestByWeek`, `currentWeekDigest`) uses the legacy `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()` / `identity.subject` pattern instead of `requireAuth(ctx)`. This is flagged in project memory as a HIGH issue: 60+ instances still need migrating.

findings.title: weeklySummaries.ts uses raw ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() ‚Äî legacy auth pattern
findings.description: All four handlers in `weeklySummaries.ts` (`add`, `getLatest`, `getByWeek`, `listAll`) use the legacy auth pattern. Same issue as F004.
findings.fix: Same migration as F004 ‚Äî use `requireAuth(ctx)` from `./lib/auth`.

findings.title: conversations.ts partially migrated ‚Äî addUserMessage, addAssistantMessage, list, listByReport, listByDateRange, search still use legacy auth
findings.description: `claimPendingReplies` and `pendingReplies` correctly use `requireAuth(ctx)`, but the remaining six handlers use `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()` directly. Mixed auth patterns in the same file are confusing and error-prone.
findings.fix: Migrate all remaining handlers in `conversations.ts` to use `requireAuth(ctx)` and remove the legacy pattern from this file.

findings.title: backfillFoodTrials and backfillWeeklyDigests use legacy auth pattern
findings.description: The two public-facing `mutation` exports `backfillFoodTrials` (line 744) and `backfillWeeklyDigests` (line 800) and `backfillKnownFoods` (line 961) use the legacy `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()` pattern. The `internalMutation` variants are fine (they accept a `userId` arg), but the public entry-points should use `requireAuth`.
findings.fix: Replace `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()` / `identity.subject` in the three public mutations with `const { userId } = await requireAuth(ctx)`.

findings.title: Date.now() used inside an internalMutation (updateWeeklyDigest)
findings.description: `updateWeeklyDigestImpl` uses `Date.now()` on line 605 (`updatedAt: Date.now()`) inside a function called from an `internalMutation`. Convex mutations must be deterministic ‚Äî they are replayed on failure. `Date.now()` will return a different value on replay, making the `updatedAt` field non-deterministic and potentially incorrect. The same issue exists in `waitlist.ts` lines 49 and 82.
findings.fix:
Pass `recomputeAt` (already computed on line 326 and available in the digest context) as the `updatedAt` value instead of `Date.now()`:
```ts
updatedAt: args.eventTimestamp, // or derive a recomputeAt from loaded data
```
For `waitlist.ts`, accept `subscribedAt`/`unsubscribedAt` as args from the action layer, or use `ctx._creationTime` as a deterministic anchor.

findings.title: Date.now() used inside mutations in waitlist.ts
findings.description: `subscribedAt: Date.now()` (line 49 and 66) and `unsubscribedAt: Date.now()` (line 82) are called inside mutations. Mutations are replayed by Convex on failure; `Date.now()` will differ across replays, producing non-deterministic timestamps. This is a Convex correctness violation.

findings.title: currentWeekDigest uses new Date() and wall-clock arithmetic inside a query
findings.description: `currentWeekDigest` (line 152-173) uses `new Date()` and `now.getDay()` to compute the current Monday inside a Convex `query`. Queries must be deterministic and referentially transparent ‚Äî the same query with the same DB state must always return the same result. Using wall-clock time in a query means it silently changes behavior at midnight on Monday without any data change triggering a re-evaluation. This also makes the query non-cacheable and non-testable.

findings.title: Date.now() used as fallback anchor in internalMutation (updateFoodTrialSummaryImpl)
findings.description: Line 316: `const windowAnchor = aiAnalysis?.timestamp ?? Date.now();`. This is inside `updateFoodTrialSummaryImpl` which is called from an `internalMutation`. If `aiAnalysis` is null (e.g., the ID was just written and the mutation reads before it commits ‚Äî unlikely but possible in edge cases), the fallback `Date.now()` makes the mutation non-deterministic.

findings.title: suggestionsByDateRange uses .collect() with no limit ‚Äî potential full-scan
findings.description: Wait, this function is in `aiAnalyses.ts`. `suggestionsByDateRange` (line 138) calls `.collect()` on `aiAnalyses` filtered by a timestamp range with no cap. For a user with many AI analyses over a broad date range, this could read thousands of documents in a single query. The function is also in a file named `aiAnalyses.ts`, not `aggregateQueries.ts`.
findings.fix: Add a `.take(limit)` with a reasonable cap (e.g., 500) and document the constraint. Alternatively, store suggestions in their own index-able table if this query becomes a hot path.

findings.title: latestSuccessful does a take(100) then filters in JS ‚Äî fragile if user has many failed analyses
findings.description: `latestSuccessful` takes the 100 most recent rows then does `rows.find(...)` in memory. If the user's last 100 analyses are all failures, the function returns null even though a successful analysis exists further back. This produces a silent, incorrect result rather than an explicit error.
findings.fix:
Either:
1. Add a dedicated index on `(userId, error)` or `(userId, hasInsight)` if the table is large enough to warrant it.
2. Or increase the take limit and document the assumption, with an explicit warning if none is found after scanning N rows:
```ts
// If all 100 are failures, log a warning so it surfaces in monitoring
if (!row) console.warn(`[latestSuccessful] No successful analysis found in last 100 for user ${userId}`);
```

findings.title: listByReport fetches all conversations by aiAnalysisId then filters by userId in JS
findings.description:
The `listByReport` query uses the `by_aiAnalysisId` index (line 93) which is not scoped by `userId`. It fetches all messages for a given `aiAnalysisId` across all users, then filters by `userId` in JavaScript (line 98). This means any user who guesses a valid `aiAnalysisId` will cause the server to read all messages for that analysis before the filter discards them. It is not a data-exposure bug (the filter is applied before returning), but it is an unnecessary read of other users' data rows.

The correct fix is to use a `by_userId_aiAnalysisId` compound index so the DB query returns only that user's messages.
findings.fix:
Add a compound index `by_userId_aiAnalysisId` on the `conversations` table in `schema.ts`, then replace the query:
```ts
.withIndex('by_userId_aiAnalysisId', q =>
  q.eq('userId', userId).eq('aiAnalysisId', args.aiAnalysisId)
)
.collect()
```
This eliminates the in-memory filter and prevents cross-user row reads.

findings.title: scheduleMissingExtractionsForUser fires N scheduler.runAfter(0) calls in a tight loop
findings.description: `scheduleMissingExtractionsForUser` loops over up to 200 analyses and calls `ctx.scheduler.runAfter(0, ...)` for each one that needs extraction. All tasks are scheduled with delay 0, meaning they all try to execute simultaneously. For a user with many unprocessed analyses this creates a burst of concurrent mutations, which can produce lock contention on shared records (profile, foodTrialSummary) and exceed Convex's per-second mutation rate.
findings.fix:
Stagger the scheduled tasks using the same `BACKFILL_STAGGER_MS` pattern used in `backfillFoodTrialsWorker`:
```ts
await ctx.scheduler.runAfter(
  processed * BACKFILL_STAGGER_MS,
  internal.extractInsightData.extractFromReport,
  { aiAnalysisId: analysis._id },
);
```

findings.title: backfillFoodTrialsWorker collects ALL foodAssessments for the user with no limit
findings.description: `backfillFoodTrialsWorker` calls `.collect()` on `foodAssessments` filtered only by `userId` (line 661-663). For a user with thousands of assessments this will read every single one in one mutation to build the `reportIds` set. This is likely to approach or exceed Convex's 32K document read limit for power users.
findings.fix: Paginate this using a cursor-based approach (similar to `backfillKnownFoodsWorker`) or use a `.take(limit)` with a documented constraint. Because the goal is just to enumerate distinct `aiAnalysisId` values, it is also worth adding an index on `(userId, aiAnalysisId)` if one does not already exist and querying distinct IDs more efficiently.

findings.title: Waitlist join mutation has no rate limiting or email format validation
findings.description: The `join` mutation accepts any email string up to 254 characters and is callable without authentication (no auth check). There is no email format validation (a Convex validator only checks type, not format) and no rate-limiting mechanism. A script could flood the waitlist table with garbage entries.
findings.fix:
1. Add basic email format validation with a regex before the upsert.
2. Consider adding a honeypot or rate-limit field (e.g., track the number of inserts from a given IP ‚Äî though Convex does not expose IP natively, this may need a Convex HTTP action with Cloudflare rate limiting in front).
3. At minimum validate the email has an `@` and a dot in the domain:
```ts
if (!/^[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+$/.test(email)) throw new Error('Invalid email address');
```

findings.title: No server-side AI rate limiting ‚Äî a user can call chatCompletion unlimited times
findings.description: The `chatCompletion` action has no rate limiting or per-user call tracking. A user (or compromised session) can call this in a tight loop, consuming their own API key quota or, if the server-stored key path is taken, exhausting a shared key. There is no tracking of usage in this file, and no guard against repeated calls.

findings.title: model arg is unconstrained ‚Äî client can request any OpenAI model
findings.description: The `model` parameter is a free-form `v.string()` with no allowlist. A client could request expensive models (`gpt-4o`, `o1-preview`, etc.) or invalid model strings. The server has no way to enforce which models users are permitted to use.

findings.title: Client-supplied apiKey accepted in args ‚Äî creates a key-laundering surface
findings.description: The action accepts `apiKey: v.string()` from the client and uses it as the OpenAI key. This means any authenticated user can pass an arbitrary API key (including stolen or shared keys) through the server's OpenAI call path. The design intention appears to be BYOK (bring your own key) where the client sends the key, but this conflates BYOK with a server-relay pattern in a way that could be used to proxy calls through your infrastructure with a third-party's key.
findings.fix: If BYOK is intended, document this explicitly and add logging of the key suffix used per call so abuse can be detected. Consider making the `apiKey` arg optional and only usable if the user has no server-stored key ‚Äî i.e., enforce a priority: server-stored key > client-supplied key, which is the reverse of the current `prefer client-provided` logic. Review `feedback_llm_key_architecture.md` for the original BYOK decision.

findings.title: upsertFoodTrialSummaries deleteOrphans=true branch has dead-code duplicate
findings.description: Lines 189-199: when `deleteOrphans` is true, both branches of the inner `if (expectedCanonicalNames.has(canonicalName))` execute the same `ctx.db.delete(summary._id)` statement. The two branches have different comments but identical effect ‚Äî the conditional is dead code and the comment about 'stale alias duplicate' vs 'orphan' distinction is meaningless in this path.

findings.title: latestAiVerdict ternary is a no-op passthrough for non-avoid/non-trial_next values
findings.fix: Remove the redundant ternary: `const latestAiVerdict = summary.latestAiVerdict;`

findings.title: list query limit clamps to 1000 ‚Äî document why 1000 is safe
findings.description: The `list` query clamps the limit to `Math.min(Math.max(args.limit ?? 50, 1), 1000)`. 1000 aiAnalysis rows is not inherently dangerous, but it should be documented why this ceiling is acceptable relative to Convex's 32K document read limit. If each row is large, 1000 may still cause performance issues.
findings.fix: Add a comment explaining the limit justification, similar to the WQ-335 comment pattern used in `computeAggregates.ts`.

findings.title: Stripe integration uses raw fetch instead of the Stripe SDK
findings.description: The Stripe checkout session is created via a raw `fetch` call with manually encoded form body. The official Stripe Node.js SDK handles encoding, error parsing, retry logic, and idempotency keys automatically. The raw fetch approach is fragile and more error-prone to maintain.
